title: Needle-felted frog
subtitle: A squat green frog in merino with textured skin and amber eyes
excerpt: Build a compact common frog in olive and mid-green merino, using layers of needle felting to achieve the slightly bumpy skin texture. The finished frog sits about 6 cm across and makes a cheerful desk companion or gift.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. Compact-animal forms are a core British needle-felting beginner project; the body-building sequence follows standard guild workshop technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to tangle wool fibres together without heat or water. Each stab locks fibres into a progressively firmer mass.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon. The standard input material for both needle and wet felting.

content.text: is built from a rounded body, four short legs, and small bumps on the back. Use olive merino
for the main colour. Add a mid-green layer on the back and pale cream on the belly.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees onto the foam mat only. Wear a finger guard on the supporting hand.

content.text: Pull off about 12 g of olive roving and gather it into a rough ball. Place it on the foam mat and stab repeatedly with the size-38 needle, rotating the ball every few passes to build a squat sphere about 5 cm in diameter. Compress the underside slightly by stabbing the base flat. Felt until the surface is firm and smooth.

Tease a thin layer of mid-green roving and lay it over the upper half of the body. Stab it in with the size-40 needle, keeping it thin so the olive base shows through at the sides. Apply pale cream over the underside, stabbing it flat. The boundary between back and belly should be gradual, not a hard line.

A frog has a wide, flat-fronted head almost continuous with the body. Add a slightly raised mound of olive roving over the front quarter, stab it smooth and flat, then define a horizontal mouth groove with a few deliberate passes of the size-40 needle.

For each front leg, roll a short sausage of olive roving about 3 cm long and felt it firm, then attach it to the front sides of the body just below the head. For the back legs, shape two wider pieces about 4 cm long, bent at a knee. Felt each back leg to the lower sides of the body so they angle outward and slightly backward.

Roll two balls of dark brown merino about 6 mm in diameter. Felt each one until firm, then position them high on the head, wide apart and slightly protruding. Stab from several angles to secure. Add a tiny amber spot to each eye.

Use the size-40 needle to raise small bumps across the back by gathering tiny pinches of surface fibres and stabbing them into raised dots. Space the bumps 5 to 8 mm apart in an irregular pattern. Leave the head and underside smooth.

items.problem: The body is circular rather than squat
items.cause: Even pressure all around creates a sphere; frogs need a flattened base
items.fix: Place the body base-down and stab horizontally into the lower sides to flatten the base and widen the silhouette.

items.problem: The legs do not hold their position
items.cause: The joint area was not felted deeply enough through to the body core
items.fix: Use longer, deeper strokes at the joint, angled through both the limb and the body. Add a small amount of roving over each joint and felt it in.
